coredata.dc:title: Highly stable carbon nanodot-based phosphor as a color converter for WLED
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Guowei
Bai, Ying
Yu, Caiyan
Xuan, Tongtong
coredata.dc:description: The core bottleneck problem of solid-state carbon nanodots (CDs) is aggregation-induced luminescence quenching (AIQ) effects. Herein, we report an approach to obtain a strong fluorescence emission CD@monodisperse mesoporous silica nanosphere-stellate (CD@MSNS) hybrid phosphor with highly concentrated emitting centers, where the CDs are effectively attached to the MSNS particles through physical adsorption and hydrogen bonding. The effective adsorption between CDs and MSNS could increase the distance between CDs, which could steadily hinder the AIQ effect of CDs. The highlight of this method is to prevent the aggregation of CDs upon drying. The obtained CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor possesses excellent thermal-stability and photostability. A white light-emitting diode (WLED) with a luminous efficiency of 40.6 lm/W, CIE coordinates of (0.3003, 0.3731), and color rendering index (CRI) of 80.9 is fabricated based on monochromatic converter of the yellow CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor. This study provides a new vision to solve the AIQ effects of quantum nanodots and offers a simple method for CDs application in WLED.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nano-dots
CD-Based hybrid phosphor
Aggregation-induced luminescence quenching (AIQ) effects
WLED

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the formation mechanism for a CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor.
PL spectra of (a) CDs and (c) CD@MSNS. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, MSNS, and CD@MSNS. (d) PL intensity of CDs, MSNS, and CD@MSNS under excitation of 450 nm.
(a) XRD patterns of CDs and CD@MSNS, TEM images of (b) CDs, (c) MSNS, and (d) CD@MSNS.
(a) XPS, (b) Raman, and (c) FTIR spectra of CDs, MSNS, and CD@MSNS.
PL lifetime of (a) CDs and (b) CD@MSNS. The fluorescence intensity curve of CD@MSNS (c) heated to series temperatures and (d) irradiated for different times under a 455 nm blue-lamp.
The electroluminescence spectra and photograph (inset) of the as-fabricated WLED operated at a current of 40 mA.

head.ce:title: Highly stable carbon nanodot-based phosphor as a color converter for WLED

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, School of Physics & Electronics, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, School of Physics & Electronics, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surface, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surface

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surface, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The core bottleneck problem of solid-state carbon nanodots (CDs) is aggregation-induced luminescence quenching (AIQ) effects. Herein, we report an approach to obtain a strong fluorescence emission CD@monodisperse mesoporous silica nanosphere-stellate (CD@MSNS) hybrid phosphor with highly concentrated emitting centers, where the CDs are effectively attached to the MSNS particles through physical adsorption and hydrogen bonding. The effective adsorption between CDs and MSNS could increase the distance between CDs, which could steadily hinder the AIQ effect of CDs. The highlight of this method is to prevent the aggregation of CDs upon drying. The obtained CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor possesses excellent thermal-stability and photostability. A white light-emitting diode (WLED) with a luminous efficiency of 40.6 lm/W, CIE coordinates of (0.3003, 0.3731), and color rendering index (CRI) of 80.9 is fabricated based on monochromatic converter of the yellow CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor. This study provides a new vision to solve the AIQ effects of quantum nanodots and offers a simple method for CDs application in WLED.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their remarkable characteristics, for instance, ultralong life, low energy consumption, and high efficiency, white light-emitting diodes (WLED) illumination has recently drawn a booming concern in the lighting field. The combination of blue LED chips or ultraviolet (UV) chips and a relevant phosphor is the representative disposition for WLED. Large-scale applications of conventional rare-earth phosphor in WLED are restricted by nonrenewable raw material sources and high energy consumption during the production process [1–3]. Therefore, the exploration of new phosphors boosts the development of WLED and the corresponding performance optimization. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been proposed as promising alternatives to rare-earth phosphor and present bright prospects in WLED, because of their low cost, nontoxicity, and favorable fluorescence [4–6]. Nevertheless, the self-quenching derived from the aggregation of CDs and the poor thermal stability limit the performance of CDs in WLED applications. With respect to the unsatisfied thermalstability and aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effect, it is imminent to adopt facile, new and efficient methods to solve these problems so that they can meet practical needs [7,8].

To solve foregoing shortcomings of CDs, it is often necessary to carry out some post-processing processing. The most commonly adopted solution is surface passivation, but the purification process is complicated [9,10]. Another effective strategy is matrix material encapsulation, and, the fluorescence of CDs is protected via dispersion in matrix materials. Benefiting from this factor, CDs overcome fluorescence quenching. Therefore, embedding CDs into solid matrices, such as polymers [11], AlOOH [12], silicates [13], and inorganic salts [14], has been adopted to retain the fluorescence. Although these above strategies satisfactorily solve the solid-state fluorescence quenching of CDs, certain limitations are observed especially for the poor thermal stability. As a result, CDs still face the difficulty of achieving breakthroughs in practical applications of optoelectronic devices [15–17]. Thus, developing an effective method to restrain fluorescence quenching together with making compounds with high thermal stability is necessary to realize high performance in practical applications. To achieve this mission, the selection of a suitable matrix material is crucial because it not only affects the dispersion of CDs but also determines the thermal stability of the composite material. Among the various matrix materials, monodisperse mesoporous silica nanosphere-stellate (MSNS) should be a promising candidate due to its low cost, excellent thermostability, strong oxidation resistance, non-toxic and odorless features. Moreover, when dispersed into the medium, MSNS could effectively adsorb fluorescent particles and enhance the photoluminescence (PL) strength. Therefore, if MSNS is introduced into CDs, it could not only act as the adsorption matrix material to suppress the AIQ effect of CDs but also improve the thermal stability and enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

Herein, we conducted a facile hydrothermal method by using polyethylene glycol, 1,2-diaminobenzene, and deionized water as raw materials to obtain CDs solution with strong yellow emitting (Fig. 1 ). Then, an MSNS matrix is dispersed in the CDs solution in a well-proportioned manner to retain the fluorescence.

With this method, the abovementioned problems are successfully resolved to endow the excellent stability of a CD-based phosphor under high temperature conditions. In addition, the resultant hybrid phosphor (CD@MSNS) exhibits excellent photostability, and the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) reaches 62.5%, which is higher than that in previous reports. These merits allow for the fabrication of CD-based WLED by using the CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor as a color conversion layer. Combined with excellent thermal stability and photochemical stability, a white light-emitting diode (WLED) with a luminous efficiency of 40.6 lm/W, color rendering index (CRI) of 80.9, and CIE coordinates of (0.3003, 0.3731) is fabricated based on a monochromatic converter of the yellow CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor. This research provides a facile and controllable process for CDs applications in WLED and opens up a new path for solving the AIQ effects of quantum dots (QDs).

MSNS was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. Polyethylene glycol (Mw = 4000, abbreviated as PEG-4K) and 1,2-diaminobenzene were bought at the Shanghai Aladdin Biochem Technology Co., Ltd. The transparent silicone resin, and the blue LED chips dedicated for WLED packaging were acquired from Dalian Xintuo Optoelectronics Co., Ltd. All raw materials were used as received without any additional treatment.

Synthesis of Solid-state CD@MSNS: (1) 0.1 g PEG-4K and 0.5 g 1,2-diaminobenzene were initially distributed in 75 mL purified water under vigorous stirring. After moving the resultant solution into the polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, it was kept at 180 °C for 10 h and naturally cooled to room-temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Finally, a light brown CD solution was acquired for further analysis. (2) 10 mL CD solution and 10 g MSNS were transferred into a flask which containing 100 mL deionized water under stirring for 0.5 h. The obtained mixture was off-centered at 4000 rpm for 5 min. Finally, the CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor was acquired after the sediment was evaporated at 80 °C for 1 h.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra, photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were measured by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FluoroMax-4) containing an integrating sphere unit (Horiba Jobin Yvon, F3029). In addition, the BaSO4 slice was taken as reference. A UV/visible (UV/Vis) spectrophotometer (Ultrafast Systems, Helios-EOS FIRE) was adopted to analyze the absorption characteristics of the obtained samples. An D8 Advance XRD (Bruker) was utilized to characterize the phase structure. A RM-1000 Raman spectrometer were employed to analyze the characteristic carbon Raman peaks of the as-prepared samples. A Tecnai G2 20 TEM was used to research the micro-structure. A multifunction imaging photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, AXIS ULTRA) was adopted to measure the element compositions and valence state of the obtained samples. A NICOLET 5700 FT-IR spectrometer was employed to record the characteristic FTIR spectra. The electroluminescence properties of the as-fabricated WLED were investigated by an APMS-80 spectrophotometer.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of CDs and CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor were tested to analyze their intrinsic fluorescence characteristics. The CDs show typical excitation-independent emission features, with the strongest emission peak located at 551 nm under excitation of 450 nm (Fig. 2a). The CDs solution (10 mL) to mass ratio of MSNS to was adjusted from 10:1, 10:5, 10:10, 10:30, to 10:50 to optimize PL properties of CD-based phosphors (Fig. S1), respectively. Obviously, the optimal mass of MSNS was 10 g, the ratio was 1:1 (CD@MSNS). Fig. 2b presents the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, MSNS, and CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor. The absorption spectrum of the CDs contained two typical UV peaks (250 and 292 nm) and one broad visible spectrum peak at 430 nm. The π-π* transitions of the aromatic sp [2] domain and the n-π* transitions of CN/CO and/or C–N are the origin for the absorption peak in the ultraviolet region and the visible absorption band, respectively [18–20]. In contrast, because of the absence of CN and C–N bonds, MSNS has no absorption peak in the visible light region. Meanwhile, the CD@MSNS phosphor has a stronger absorption than the CD solution ranging from 300–500 nm. This results demonstrates that the CDs are successfully adsorbed on the surface of the MSNS. It is important to note that MSNS does not compete with the absorbed excitation light and reabsorbs emission from CDs, confirming the MSNS is an ideal matrix material. Therefore, a CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor with enhanced fluorescence intensity is obtained. After the integration of CDs and MSNS, the emission bands of the CD@MSNS phosphor (Fig. 2c) exhibit a blue shift from 551 nm to 532 nm under 450 nm excitation. The PLQY improves from 36.9% to 62.5% of the CD solution. Moreover, the PL intensity is further enhanced after integrating CDs with MSNS (Fig. 2d). The above-mentioned results confirmed that the combination of CDs and MSNS could effectively inhibit the AIQ effect. Inspiring, a new route for preparing economically effective and eco-friendly carbon-based hybrid phosphors was found.

XRD identifies the phase compositions of CDs and the CD@MSNS phosphor, as presented in Fig. 3 a. Due to the intrinsic noncrystalline properties of MSNS, the XRD pattern of MSNS does not exist. The broad diffraction peak (21.7°) of CDs manifests their weak crystallinity [21,22]. The CD@MSNS phosphor also exhibits a diffraction peak at 21.7°. It should be noted that MSNS is an amorphous material, so there is no XRD characteristic peak, demonstrating that the integration of CDs and MSNS has no significant effect on the crystal structure of CDs. The TEM image of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 3b) shows an oblatoid morphology, distributed within 1.2–5.5 nm. The TEM images displayed in Fig. 3c and d indicate that the adsorption binding of CDs and MSNS hardly affects the size and micro-morphology of MSNS due to the considerably small size of CDs.

Therefore, this work does not change the morphology of the matrix, whose presence is equivalent to the dispersing agent, which acquires uniform dispersion and finally suppresses the AIQ effect of CDs. Moreover, after the integration of CDs and MSNS, the surface area and pore volume of MSNS vary from 171.059 m2/g and 0.778 cc/g to 1821.689 m2/g and 0.632 cc/g, respectively. The BET results confirm that the CDs are successfully adsorbed on MSNS.

The XPS technique was employed to further probe the chemical composition of the samples. The binding energy of C 1s at 284.8 eV was adopted as a is adjusted reference. The characteristic peaks consistent with Si 2p (102.96 eV), C 1s (284.79 eV), N 1s (402.35 eV), and O 1s (532.28 eV) are observed in the XPS survey spectrum of the CD@MSNS phosphor (Fig. 4 a) [23–25], demonstrating the existence of Si in the CD@MSNS phosphor with 17.64 at.% Si, 42.2 at.% C, 2.48 at.% N, and 37.86 at.% O. Meanwhile, the atom ratios of C, O, and N in CDs are 74.956%, 17.411%, and 7.633%, respectively, verifying the successful integration of CDs and MSNS.

The Raman spectra are measured to further in-depth research the characteristic vibration peak of MSNS, CDs, and CD@MSNS phosphor (Fig. 4b). The Raman spectra of CDs present one broad peak locked at 1352 cm−1, which is ascribed to the D band that represents the disordered structure and lattice defects of graphite [26]. Given that the location of the Raman characteristic peak of SiO2 is similar to that of CDs [27], the characteristic peak of the CD@MSNS phosphor does not change much.

The FTIR spectra (Fig. 4c) further manifest that massive oxygen-containing groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amide, and carbonyl, exist on the surface of CDs [28,29]. These groups endow the CDs with excellent water solubility. The FTIR characteristic peaks located at 821 cm−1 (Si–O), 971 cm−1 (Si–OH), and 1076 cm−1 (Si–O–Si) were attributed to MSNS, which is consistent with previous reports [30,31]. CDs and the CD@MSNS phosphor show the same vibration peaks, except for the Si–O–Si peaks of 926 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1. The formation mechanism of the CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor was summarized based on the above systematic analysis, namely, that CDs effectively adsorbed on the surface of MSNS through surface hydrogen bonding and physical interaction.

The PLQY and fluorescent lifetime (τ) of CDs and CD@MSNS phosphor are measured to deeply investigate the PL strengthening mechanism for the CD-based hybrid phosphor. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a and 5b, the PL decay curves are matched with mono-exponential decay. Clearly, the value of τ significantly increases after CDs integrate with MSNS, confirming that CDs could evenly disperse on the exterior of MSNS. The graphitizing cores cannot directly contact with each other owing to the prominent steric hindrance of the MSNS (Fig. 1), and thus, the CDs maintain an appropriate interval (within the Förster distance) between their inner particles. This may be the most important factor for efficient solid-state fluorescence, because the π-π interaction, a major energy-transfer process responsible for non-radiative decay process and self-quenching, could be effectively avoided by taking advantage of it [32–39].

In addition to fluorescence intensity, remarkable thermal stability and photo-stability are also crucial for LED applications because their working temperature could reach 150 °C. Hence, the stability of the CD@MSNS phosphor for WLED packaging is investigated (Fig. 5c). The normalized PL intensity and appearance remained unchanged after storage for 60 days in air at room temperature (Fig. S2). The normalized PL intensity change of the CD@MSNS phosphor at different temperatures demonstrates that no common PL decay is appears for the CD@MSNS phosphor from 25 °C to 250 °C. Conversely, the PL intensity reaches its highest maule at 175 °C, and reach to 166% of that at room-temperature. Subsequently, although the PL intensity decreases with increasing temperature, it is still equivalent to the intensity of CDs at room-temperature. Similarly, there was almost no reduction in PL intensity for the CD@MSNS phosphor with time prolonged as long as 10 h under a blue lamp of 455 nm irradiation, suggesting an excellent photo-stability of the phosphor. In conclusion, the resultant CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor is an ideal material for WLED.

Then, 0.5 g silicone resin, 0.25 g CD@MSNS phosphor, and 0.5 g anhydride curing agent were evenly mixed by grinding. Then, the obtained slurry was directly coated onto a 455 nm blue LED chips. Hereafter, the resultant chips are thermally cured in an oven at 150 °C for 1 h to form a luminescent layer. Ultimately, a WLED device with superior performances that adopted yellow-emission CD@MSNS phosphors were packaged.

As displayed in Fig. 6 , the WLED based on the CD@MSNS phosphor has a wide spectrum ranging from 400 to 700 nm, almost overlaying the whole visible region. The emission peaks at 450 nm and 541 nm are attributed to the blue LED chips and CD@MSNS phosphor, respectively. The emission peak of the CD@MSNS phosphor (Fig. 2c, 532 nm) displays a red shift of 9 nm after mixing with silicone resin. Driven by a direct current of 40 mA, the WLED possess bright white light with CRI, CCT, and luminous efficacy of 80.9, 5117 K, and 40.6 lm/W, respectively. The CIE coordinates of (0.3003, 0.3731) is in close proximity to that of pure white light of (0.33, 0.33). The EL properties of WLED could be adjusted by changing the CD content in the CD@MSNS hybrid phosphor to meet certain special application standards. In conclusion, the CDs-based hybrid phosphor is a potential candidate for commercial WLED applications.

The adsorption of CDs onto MSNS effectively alleviates the AIQ effect of CDs and provides an adjustable fluorescence characteristics of the hybrid phosphor. Moreover, the WLED application is demonstrated by combining the CD@MSNS phosphor and commercial blue LEDs without other phosphors. This work gives rise to some enlightenment for solving the stuck neck conundrum of CDs in practical lighting and display areas. CDs-based phosphor will not only brighten the foreground of new generation frontier science and technology of CDs optoelectronics but also provide a substitution for expensive rare-earth phosphor and toxic semiconductor quantum dots.

Guowei Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft; Ying Bai: Funding acquisition, Resources, Conceptualization, Formal analysis; Caiyan Yu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing; Tongtong Xuan: Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (52072112, 52002115, and 51672069), the Zhongyuan Thousand Talents Program of Henan Province (ZYQR201912155), the Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in University of Henan Province (IRTSTHN) (20IRTSTHN012), the Foundation of Henan Educational Committee (20A140004) and Science and Technology Development Project of Henan Province (202102210105, 212102210487), Henan Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (CXJD2021003).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2022.118836.
